memes.label: About This Wiki

memes.label: 'Table of Contents'
memes.description: 'Table of Contents'

memes.label: Al Blanchard
memes.description: Jack Keefe's best friend from Bedford, Indiana, and the recipient of all his letters throughout the "You Know Me Al" stories.

memes.label: Allen
memes.description: Left-handed pitcher, Jack Keefe's roommate during spring training, and later his brother-in-law through marriage to Florrie's sister Marie.

memes.label: American League
memes.description: The major league in which Jack Keefe's Chicago White Sox compete — the younger of baseball's two major leagues, founded in 1901.

memes.label: Babe Ruth
memes.description: The legendary slugger who began as a star pitcher — Jack Keefe recounts striking out Ruth twice, a claim that captures both Jack's pride and the transitional moment in baseball history that Ruth represents.

memes.label: Base Hit
memes.description: The fundamental offensive achievement in baseball — reaching base safely by hitting the ball — discussed by Jack Keefe in the context of luck, skill, and scorekeeping disputes.

memes.label: Bedford, Indiana
memes.description: Jack Keefe's hometown in Indiana, where his friend Al Blanchard still lives — the destination of all his letters and the yardstick against which he measures his big-league success.

memes.label: Bench
memes.description: The seating area where players wait their turn, and the state of being removed from the game — both physical location and professional status in Jack Keefe's baseball world.

memes.label: Boston, Massachusetts
memes.description: Major American city and home of the Boston Red Sox, a frequent opponent in Jack Keefe's pitching career and one of the cities on the American League circuit.

memes.label: Boston Red Sox
memes.description: The American League team featuring Babe Ruth (as a pitcher) and Tris Speaker — another powerhouse opponent on Jack Keefe's schedule.

memes.label: Bunt
memes.description: A strategic play in baseball where the batter taps the ball lightly into the infield, often used to advance runners or surprise the defense.

memes.label: Charley Comiskey
memes.description: The owner of the Chicago White Sox, a real historical figure who appears in Jack Keefe's letters as the dispenser of contracts, praise, and the occasional handshake.

memes.label: Chicago Cubs
memes.description: National League baseball club and the Chicago White Sox's cross-town rival, a frequent antagonist in Jack Keefe's letters.

memes.label: Chicago, Illinois
memes.description: The city where the Chicago White Sox play and Jack Keefe's married home — the center of his professional and domestic life.

memes.label: Chicago White Sox
memes.description: The American League baseball team that signs Jack Keefe — the center of his professional world and the subject of his pride, complaints, and ambitions.

memes.label: Chick Gandil
memes.description: A White Sox first baseman who appears in Jack Keefe's letters as a teammate whose contract disputes and professional grievances mirror Jack's own.

memes.label: Clubhouse
memes.description: The team's private dressing and gathering space at the ballpark, where Jack Keefe receives praise, criticism, visitors, and the occasional physical confrontation.

memes.label: Comiskey Park
memes.description: The Chicago White Sox ballpark, opened in 1910 by Charley Comiskey — the stage for Jack Keefe's major league performances.
aliases: the Baseball Palace of the World

memes.label: Contract
memes.description: The legal agreement binding a player to a team, which Jack Keefe negotiates with a mixture of bluster, naivety, and pride throughout his letters.

memes.label: Curve Ball
memes.description: A breaking pitch that drops or moves laterally as it approaches the batter, frequently discussed by Jack Keefe in his letters.

memes.label: Detroit, Michigan
memes.description: Major American city and home of the Detroit Tigers, a division rival of the Chicago White Sox and the team Jack Keefe once hoped to be traded to.

memes.label: Detroit Tigers
memes.description: The American League team featuring Ty Cobb and Sam Crawford — the most formidable opponent Jack Keefe faces and the team that exposes his weaknesses.

memes.label: Double Header
memes.description: Two baseball games played back-to-back on the same day, a scheduling practice that Jack Keefe experiences with both exhaustion and wry humor throughout his letters.

memes.label: Ed Walsh
memes.description: Hall of Fame pitcher for the Chicago White Sox, the veteran ace of the staff during Jack Keefe's early years with the club, known for his spitball, his singing voice, and his willingness to mentor — and needle — the young busher.

memes.label: Eddie Cicotte
memes.description: Right-handed pitcher for the Chicago White Sox, a key member of the pitching rotation alongside Jack Keefe, and later infamous as a central figure in the 1919 Black Sox scandal.

memes.label: Eddie Collins
memes.description: Second baseman and star hitter for the Chicago White Sox, regarded as one of the best batters on the team during Jack Keefe's years with the club.

memes.label: Fast Ball
memes.description: Jack Keefe's primary pitch — a high-velocity delivery that "hops" when thrown well, central to his identity as a pitcher.

memes.label: Federal League
memes.description: A short-lived "outlaw" major league (1914–1915) that briefly challenged the American and National Leagues' monopoly, mentioned in Jack Keefe's letters as a potential suitor.

memes.label: Florrie Keefe
memes.description: Jack Keefe's wife, whose practicality, jealousy, and unexpected baseball knowledge make her one of the most vivid supporting characters in the letters.

memes.label: Fly Ball
memes.description: A batted ball hit high into the air, one of the most common outcomes in baseball and a recurring element in Jack Keefe's game narratives.

memes.label: Fungo
memes.description: A practice drill where a coach or player hits fly balls to fielders using a lightweight "fungo bat," frequently referenced in Jack Keefe's accounts of training and pre-game routines.

memes.label: George Weaver
memes.description: A White Sox infielder and Jack Keefe's teammate, whose fielding — both brilliant and erratic — is a recurring subject of Jack's game accounts.

memes.label: Harry Lord
memes.description: A White Sox infielder whose defensive lapses provide Jack Keefe with a convenient scapegoat — Lord's fielding is the foil against which Jack's pitching is measured.

memes.label: Hooks Wiltse
memes.description: Left-handed pitcher for the New York Giants, encountered by Jack Keefe during the White Sox world tour, known for his sharp wit and his role in the competitive banter between the two touring clubs.

memes.label: Inning
memes.description: The fundamental unit of play in baseball — each team bats once per inning — appearing throughout Jack Keefe's letters as the clock by which his pitching performances are measured.

memes.label: Jack Fournier
memes.description: First baseman for the Chicago White Sox during Jack Keefe's tenure, a card-playing companion of Bodie, Collins, and Gleason.

memes.label: Jack Keefe
memes.description: The narrator of Ring Lardner's "You Know Me Al" — a naive, boastful, and endearing minor league pitcher who writes letters to his friend Al about his adventures in professional baseball.

memes.label: Jim Scott
memes.description: Right-handed pitcher for the Chicago White Sox, a veteran member of the starting rotation known for his "fadeaway" pitch, and a frequent point of comparison — and competition — for Jack Keefe.

memes.label: Jimmy Callahan
memes.description: The manager of the Chicago White Sox during Jack Keefe's tenure — the target of Jack's frequent complaints about strategy, pitch-calling, and being removed from games.

memes.label: Joe Benz
memes.description: A White Sox pitcher and Jack Keefe's direct rival for innings — whenever Callahan pulls Jack from a game, Benz is often the man who replaces him.

memes.label: Kid Gleason
memes.description: A veteran baseball man who serves as coach and mentor figure for the White Sox, giving Jack Keefe straightforward advice about pitching and baseball life.

memes.label: Lefty Williams
memes.description: Left-handed pitcher for the Chicago White Sox, a rotation mate of Jack Keefe during the 1910s, later infamous as one of the eight "Black Sox" players.

memes.label: Little Al
memes.description: Jack Keefe's infant son, mentioned in the later letters as a source of paternal pride and domestic comedy.

memes.label: Military Draft (World War I)
memes.description: The World War I military draft that disrupted Major League Baseball, drew several players into service, and shaped the later chapters of Jack Keefe's career.

memes.label: New York Giants
memes.description: National League baseball club based in New York City, a perennial powerhouse during the era of Jack Keefe's letters and a benchmark against which his own team measured itself.

memes.label: Outfield
memes.description: The defensive positions beyond the infield — left, center, and right field — and the players who occupy them, appearing in Jack Keefe's letters as subjects of commentary and occasional derision.

memes.label: Pennant
memes.description: The league championship of professional baseball, representing the ultimate team achievement that Jack Keefe and his teammates pursue throughout the season.

memes.label: Ping Bodie
memes.description: Outfielder for the Chicago White Sox, a colorful presence at the team dinner table, known for his inconsistent fielding and his willingness to kid and be kidded by manager Kid Gleason.

memes.label: Ray Schalk
memes.description: The Chicago White Sox catcher whose pitch-calling frustrates Jack Keefe — Schalk keeps calling for fast balls when Jack wants to throw his spitter.

memes.label: Red Faber
memes.description: Chicago White Sox pitcher and teammate of Jack Keefe, known for his effective pitching and World War I navy service.

memes.label: Ring Lardner
memes.description: The American humorist and sports columnist (1885–1933) who created Jack Keefe — Lardner's ear for vernacular speech and eye for human folly produced one of baseball's great literary creations.

memes.label: Russell
memes.description: A pitcher for the Chicago White Sox and teammate of Jack Keefe, mentioned warming up alongside the regular starters.

memes.label: Salary
memes.description: The payment a ballplayer receives for his services, which Jack Keefe discusses obsessively — comparing, demanding, rationalizing, and measuring his worth by it.

memes.label: Sam Crawford
memes.description: Detroit Tigers outfielder and slugger who appears in Jack Keefe's letters as a formidable opponent — a man Jack both fears and boasts about facing.

memes.label: San Francisco Seals
memes.description: The Pacific Coast League team to which Jack Keefe is briefly demoted — a humbling stint that tests his pride and his marriage.

memes.label: Single (Base Hit)
memes.description: The most fundamental type of base hit in baseball — a batted ball that allows the batter to reach first base safely.

memes.label: Spitter
memes.description: An illegal pitch doctored with saliva or other substances to alter its trajectory, appearing in Jack Keefe's letters as both a weapon he wields and a point of contention with his catcher.

memes.label: Strikeout
memes.description: The pitcher's ultimate triumph — retiring a batter on three strikes — which Jack Keefe recounts with pride, frustration, and the complex dynamics of pitch selection with his catcher.

memes.label: Terre Haute, Indiana
memes.description: The minor league city where Jack Keefe played before reaching the White Sox — and where he once played the outfield in a memorable brawl with teammate Burns.

memes.label: The Busher (Story Cycle)
memes.description: The recurring story cycle within "You Know Me Al" that follows Jack "The Busher" Keefe through his baseball career, from rookie to veteran.

memes.label: Trade (Baseball Player Trades)
memes.description: The recurring event of baseball player trades — a constant threat and bargaining tool in Jack Keefe's career with the Chicago White Sox.

memes.label: Tris Speaker
memes.description: The Boston Red Sox center fielder and Hall of Fame hitter, whose encounters with Jack Keefe illustrate the gap between Jack's self-image and his actual performance against elite competition.

memes.label: Ty Cobb
memes.description: The Detroit Tigers' legendary outfielder — the most feared hitter and baserunner of the dead-ball era, whose encounters with Jack Keefe are among the most vivid game sequences in the letters.

memes.label: Uniform
memes.description: The distinctive clothing worn by ballplayers, which Jack Keefe treats as a symbol of professional identity, pride, and occasionally, comic anxiety.

memes.label: United States Army
memes.description: The military service into which Jack Keefe is drafted during World War I, interrupting his baseball career and providing some of the letters' most self-reflective moments.

memes.label: Walter Johnson
memes.description: The legendary Washington Senators pitcher whose fastball was the gold standard of the dead-ball era — Jack Keefe compares himself to Johnson and measures other pitchers against him.

memes.label: Washington Senators
memes.description: The American League team anchored by Walter Johnson's legendary fastball — a benchmark opponent in Jack Keefe's baseball world.

memes.label: Wolfgang
memes.description: A member of the Chicago White Sox organization who worked out alongside Red Faber during spring training.

memes.label: World Series
memes.description: The annual championship between the American and National League pennant winners — the ultimate prize that Jack Keefe aspires to reach.

memes.label: World War I
memes.description: The global conflict (1914–1918) that interrupts Jack Keefe's baseball career when the United States enters in 1917, drafting him into military service.

memes.label: You Know Me Al
memes.description: The 1916 collection of Ring Lardner's Jack Keefe stories — six epistolary tales narrated by the naive but endearing pitcher, originally published in The Saturday Evening Post.
